text: The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is visibly scarred down in the pelvis, using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is scarred down in the pelvis, carefully unfolding it from its previous position.
The surgeon unfolds the distal segment of the bowel, which appears to be folded on itself, preparing to undo the fold as part of the Hartmann reversal procedure.
The robotic repair involves dissection below the rectovaginal septum, separating the fistula. The surgeon closes the vaginal and rectal defects and places a tongue of omentum in the area.
The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain additional length, following the initial step of taking down and stapling off the colostomy.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain length, using the colostomy site for abdominal access. A long silk thread is visible on the end of the stapled-off colostomy for identification and traction.
A long silk thread is attached to the end of the surgical instrument, aiding in identification and providing traction during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the colostomy site to access the abdomen and assesses the length of the proximal bowel.

The surgeon assesses the length of the bowel using a silk thread for traction. The tongue of omentum is visible to the right, positioned near the previous fistula site. The sigmoid loop is unfolded, increasing bowel length and eliminating tension.

The surgeon observes the silk thread used for traction and assesses the bowel length, noting the tongue of omentum positioned to the right near the previous fistula site. The sigmoid loop is unfolded, increasing bowel length and eliminating tension.
The surgeon lines up a hand-sewn anastomosis, using a stay suture made of silk at the far corner to secure the alignment.
The surgeon lines up a hand-sewn anastomosis, using a stay suture made of 3-0 silk on the far corner to secure the alignment.
The surgeon uses arm number one to hold up the bowel ends and begins sewing the two staple lines together, connecting the two ends of the bowel.
The surgeon uses robotic arm number one to hold the bowel ends in position, preparing to sew the two staple lines together to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 nine-inch V-lock suture to perform the anastomosis, demonstrating a preferred technique for joining the bowel ends.
The surgeon uses a 3-0, 9-inch V-Loc suture to perform the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates a colotomy, carefully inspecting the tissue for any signs of fusion during the incision process.

The surgeon performs a colotomy, checking for fusion during the process, and then proceeds with a proctotomy. The colotomy is made in the sigmoid colon, which was left during the initial Hartman procedure, revealing the proximal and distal openings.

The surgeon identifies the colotomy and proctotomy sites, noting the presence of sigmoid tissue left from the initial Hartmann procedure. The proximal and distal openings are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins suturing with a 3-0 V-lock suture, starting at the center of the anastomosis site and employing a baseball stitch technique to secure the tissue edges.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Lock suture, starting at the center of the surgical site, and performs a baseball stitch technique to sew around the far corner, carefully placing each suture in precise locations.
The surgeon places each suture precisely around the far corner of the surgical site, ensuring accurate positioning for closure.

The surgeon begins suturing in the middle of the incision, sewing towards themselves and cinching down each suture precisely. The suturing continues around the near corner, followed by a technique to canal over the top of the tissue.

The surgeon begins suturing in the middle of the surgical site, sewing towards themselves and cinching down each suture precisely. The suturing continues around the near corner, followed by a technique to canal over the top.
The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomy's common channel using the original inner stitch, demonstrating a sewn anastomosis technique.
The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomy's common channel using the original inner stitch, demonstrating various techniques for sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon takes the original posterior outer row of sutures and sews anteriorly across the top of the anastomosis, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon takes the original posterior outer row of sutures and sews anteriorly across the top of the anastomosis, ensuring the stitches are secure.

The surgeon performs a rigid scope examination to check the distal fistula site, followed by a leak test in the rectum and vagina, which shows no leakage. A flexible endoscopy is conducted past the anastomosis, confirming it is widely patent and well perfused. The procedure concludes with the diversion of the patient using a loop ileostomy.
